title: Gooseberry pie
subtitle: British double-crust shortcrust pie with gooseberry filling
excerpt: A classic British double-crust pie: tart gooseberries sweetened with sugar and thickened with cornflour, enclosed in a short, buttery pastry. The acidity of the berries holds up through cooking and cuts the richness of the pastry. Serve warm with cream, custard, or vanilla ice cream.
sourceNotes: Gooseberry pie has been a mainstay of British summer cooking since the seventeenth century. Gooseberries were widely cultivated in Britain and the north of England in particular developed a strong gooseberry culture. Recipes for gooseberry pie appear in Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), and in British domestic cookbooks throughout the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The double-crust shortcrust format with a sweetened gooseberry filling is consistent across these sources. The recipe here follows the established formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked — assemble the pie, wrap tightly, and freeze. Bake from frozen, adding 15 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best served on the day. Reheat leftovers in a low oven for 10 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. Start at 200°C for the first 20 minutes to set the pastry, then reduce to 180°C conventional for the final 20 to 25 minutes. The pie is done when the pastry is deep gold all over and the filling bubbles through the steam vent.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pie with both a pastry base and a pastry lid, sealed at the edge by pressing or crimping the two layers together. The lid needs a steam vent cut into it — a slit or small hole — to allow steam to escape from the hot filling; without it, the pastry puffs unevenly or the filling bubbles out at the crimped edges. Chill the assembled pie for 30 minutes before baking so the pastry is firm going into the oven.

content.text: is more forgiving than it looks. The filling goes in raw; the gooseberries cook inside the sealed pastry. Use tart, slightly underripe gooseberries — they hold their structure better and the acidity is what makes the pie worth eating. Adjust the sugar to the tartness of your fruit.

Rub the cold butter into the flour, salt, and sugar until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Add the egg yolks and cold water and mix to a firm, cohesive dough. Divide into two-thirds and one-third, press each into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Toss the gooseberries with the sugar and cornflour. Heat the oven to 200°C conventional. On a lightly floured surface, roll the larger piece of pastry and line a 23 cm pie dish, pressing into the corners and leaving an overhang. Fill with the gooseberry mixture, mounding it slightly. Roll the smaller piece of pastry into a lid. Brush the overhang with beaten egg, lay the lid on top, and press the edges together firmly. Trim the excess and crimp. Cut two small slits in the lid. Brush all over with egg wash and scatter with caster sugar.

Bake at 200°C for 20 minutes, then reduce to 180°C conventional and bake for a further 20 to 25 minutes until the pastry is deep gold all over and the filling bubbles through the steam vents. Rest for 15 minutes before slicing — the filling is very hot. Serve warm.
